cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out the answer to the question about the headline "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world." The options are A, B, C, D. Let me start by reading the article again to understand the context. The article talks about Dinara Safina, who was previously overshadowed by her brother Marat Safin. Now she's the world No.1 in women's tennis. The headline in question is from the Sydney Morning Herald during the Australian Open, and it's used to describe her volatile emotional state. The article mentions that she's as emotionally explosive as her brother, who was known for losing his temper and breaking rackets. The headline "mad as a snake" is another description of her. The question is about what the headline "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world" implies. Let me break down the options: A) She wanted to top the world in tennis when she was a baby in a basket. This seems a bit odd. The phrase "basket case" here probably doesn't refer to a literal baby in a basket. "Basket case" is an idiom meaning someone who is very nervous, unstable, or in a bad way emotionally. So A might be a trick option. B) After she won this year's Australian Open, Dinara Safina cried. The article mentions she made it to the Australian Open final and won a silver in the Olympics, but does it say she cried? The headline is about her emotional state improving from being a basket case to being top, not necessarily crying. Maybe not B. C) Too nervous at first, but Safina finally gained confidence and won. "Basket case" implies she was very nervous or unstable, and then she became the top. So this could mean she overcame her initial nervousness to succeed. The article also talks about her emotional explosiveness and how the headline refers to her volatile emotions turning into success. C might be the right answer. D) She learned from the basket case and became top. The phrase "basket case" here isn't about someone she learned from, but rather describing her own state. So D doesn't make sense. The article says that the headline by Sydney Morning Herald during the Australian Open summed up her volatile emotional state.
